text: The surgeon manages a large fifteen-centimeter myoma by placing trocars higher than usual, with a twelve-millimeter and five-millimeter trocar on the umbilicus line and the optic between the xiphoid and umbilicus. The uterine artery is controlled at its origin by opening the pericardium lateral to the infundibulopelvic ligament, allowing gas to assist in dissection. The umbilical artery and ureter are identified, and the pararectal fossa is developed through traction and counter-traction. The uterine artery is seen medially to the umbilical artery, and both pararectal and paramedical fossae are clearly visible, allowing safe placement of a clip after medialization of the left ureter.

The surgeon uses a standard clip to control the uterine arteries on the right side. The right infundibulopelvic ligament is tracked medially, and the peritoneum is opened laterally to it. The retroperitoneal space is expanded through traction and counter-traction, pushing the myoma to the left. The pararectal fossa is developed, revealing the internal iliac artery, umbilical artery, and two uterine arteries. A single clip is placed perpendicularly to control both uterine arteries, effectively devascularizing the uterus. A longitudinal incision is made medially using bipolar scissors.

The surgeon opens the uterine serosa and coagulates the myometrial capsule to expose the large myoma, maintaining a bloodless field. The myoma is clearly visible, and the incision is increased to accommodate its size. Laparoscopic ten millimeter forceps are placed laterally to the myoma to assist in extracting it from its bed. Bipolar energy is applied to the myoma when encountering large vessels.

The surgeon completes the myomectomy by removing the entire myoma from the uterus, ensuring a bloodless procedure through careful traction. The surgical field is cleaned, removing blood from the rectovaginal space. Sutures are placed using a 14mm needle with Vicryl, invaginating the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed to ensure strong scar formation and reduce postoperative bleeding. Assistants aid in invaginating the myometrium, and intracorporeal knotting is used for precise stitch control.

The surgeon demonstrates intracorporeal knotting technique to place stitches on the uterine scar, ensuring good ischemia and invagination of healthy myometrium into the myoma bed. Using a 14mm needle with Vicryl-one, the stitches are placed four centimeters lateral on both sides, achieving hemostasis and reducing the risk of intramyometrial hematoma. The final knot is placed on the posterior part of the uterine fundus, avoiding the rectum, with double flat knots for strong scar formation.

The surgeon places a large endoscopic bag, capable of holding 1200 cc, around the entire myoma to facilitate morcellation. The myoma is morcellated inside the bag using a morcellator set to 700 rods, ensuring no bowel is caught during the process. After completing morcellation, the pelvis is cleaned, and the hole used for the morcellator is closed to reduce the risk of incisional hernia.
